title: Ankara Locks Down Ahead of NATO Summit as Engine Deal, Crackdowns, and Diplomatic Maneuvers Intensify
subhead: With the July 7-8 NATO summit nine days away, Turkey arrested 178 suspects in security sweeps, saw Congress notified of a $700M engine sale for its KAAN fighter, and faced mounting criticism over journalist accreditation denials and a 13-day ban on public gatherings — all while Erdogan burnishes his diplomatic profile on Iran, Ukraine, and the Aegean.

sections.heading: Security Sweep and Public Gatherings Ban Before NATO Summit
sections.body:
Turkish authorities detained 225 people in anti-terrorism operations across Ankara and Istanbul ahead of the July 7-8 NATO summit, with 178 placed in pre-trial detention and 34 released under judicial supervision, according to Anadolu Agency citing the Ankara chief prosecutor. The raids targeted alleged members of the Islamic State and the far-left DHKP-C, but also swept up LGBTQ+ activist Yildiz Tar, editor-in-chief of Kaos GL, Revolutionary Party chair Elif Torun Oneren, environmental volunteers from the TEMA Foundation, journalists, academics, and lawyers. The Media and Law Studies Association (MLSA) said investigators asked environmental volunteers about alleged links to the banned TKP/ML, questioning whether they used code names or received armed training.

Authorities also imposed a 13-day ban on public gatherings, demonstrations, and protests from June 28 to July 10 covering summit venues and delegation hotels. The pro-Kurdish DEM Party called the detentions an effort to silence dissent, while Reporters Without Borders' Turkey representative Erol Onderoglu called Tar's detention arbitrary, saying an international summit could not justify holding a journalist on security grounds.

Separately, dozens of Turkish journalists were denied accreditation for the NATO summit, Reuters reported, with NATO spokesperson Allison Hart confirming that host nation Turkey guided decisions on press access — drawing sharp criticism from press freedom organizations.
sections.whyItMatters: The breadth of the crackdown — extending well beyond suspected militants to journalists, environmentalists, and LGBTQ+ advocates — raises questions about whether Turkey is using summit security as cover for a broader suppression of dissent, just as world leaders prepare to gather in Ankara.

sections.heading: US Formally Notifies Congress of $700M F110 Engine Sale for KAAN Fighter
sections.body:
The Trump administration formally notified Congress on June 24 of its intention to sell dozens of General Electric F110 turbofan engines to Turkey, valued at more than $700 million, Reuters reported. The package includes approximately 80 F110-GE-129 and F110-GE-129E engines that will power Turkey's indigenous KAAN fifth-generation stealth fighter — covering prototypes, developmental testing, Block 10 production, and Block 20 fighters scheduled for delivery to the Turkish Air Force between 2028 and 2030.

According to Reuters, four sources said the administration moved ahead despite objections from some lawmakers over Turkey's possession of the Russian S-400 air defense system. Representative Gregory Meeks, top Democrat on the House Foreign Affairs Committee, said the administration failed to make a good-faith effort to brief him, while Representatives Chris Pappas and Dina Titus threatened a joint resolution of disapproval. Congress has 15 days to act, though a presidential veto could override any such resolution.

The timing is significant: the sale was announced nine days before the NATO summit in Ankara. Trump, asked about the engines and a possible Turkish return to the F-35 program, said: 'I'm going to probably do something that will make them very happy.' Gonul Tol of the Middle East Institute told Reuters the engine sale was 'the lowest-hanging fruit' and that 'the real test' of US-Turkey relations lay in whether Ankara could rejoin the F-35 program — still barred under US law as long as Turkey retains the S-400s.
sections.whyItMatters: The engine sale secures propulsion for Turkey's most ambitious defense project and signals a tangible warming in US-Turkey ties, but it stops well short of resolving the S-400/F-35 standoff that remains the structural fault line in the relationship.

sections.heading: NATO Deputy Commander Pushes for Higher Defense Spending at Ankara Summit
sections.body:
NATO Deputy Supreme Allied Commander Europe Air Chief Marshal John Stringer told the Associated Press he wants the Ankara summit to spur member countries to spend more on defense, reaffirm support for Ukraine, and demonstrate alliance unity. Stringer acknowledged that the alliance is in 'one of those moments' of turbulence, citing Trump's conflicting signals on force posture in Europe and his threats to leave NATO.

The summit takes place against the backdrop of US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's six-month review of American forces in Europe, which has left European allies uncertain about future US commitments. Estonian Maj. Gen. Indrek Sirel said it remained unclear how US forces would be positioned in the Baltic states, while Finland's Brig. Gen. Jyri Raitasalo warned that changing plans 'every week or every month' undermines defense planning. Last year's NATO summit set a 3.5% of GDP defense spending target, but Britain's new Defense Secretary Dan Jarvis is grappling with a spending plan that would only reach 2.68% by 2030.

Erdogan, for his part, highlighted Turkey's role as NATO's second-largest land force and a leading contributor to alliance missions, saying Turkey wants the summit to be a 'milestone for the future of NATO's security architecture.'
sections.whyItMatters: The summit will test whether NATO can project cohesion despite Trump's unpredictability on Europe, European defense spending gaps, and Turkey's own authoritarian turn — with the host government simultaneously cracking down on dissent and press access.

sections.heading: Erdogan Positions Turkey as Regional Mediator on Iran and Ukraine
sections.body:
Speaking at a cabinet press conference on June 23, President Erdogan said Turkey 'chose diplomacy over escalation' during the US-Iran conflict, according to Daily Sabah. 'From the very first day of the attacks on Iran, we maintained a prudent, calm and diplomacy-first stance,' Erdogan said, adding that Turkey supported mediation efforts led by Pakistan alongside Qatar and Saudi Arabia.

Separately, Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan visited Moscow on June 17-18 to reiterate Turkey's offer to host Russia-Ukraine peace negotiations, according to Al-Monitor citing a diplomatic source. Fidan met with Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov and President Vladimir Putin, warning against further escalation in the Black Sea and proposing a limited ceasefire on ports and energy infrastructure. Ukraine's ambassador to Ankara told Reuters Kyiv welcomed the offer. Fidan also discussed South Caucasus developments following Armenian elections and Yerevan's peace talks with Azerbaijan.

Erdogan framed the upcoming NATO summit and an autumn Organization of Turkic States summit in Ankara as evidence of Turkey's growing diplomatic centrality, telling AK Party members that the gatherings would make Ankara 'a centre of global diplomacy.'
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is using the convergence of the Iran ceasefire, Ukraine mediation, and the NATO summit to project itself as an indispensable regional power broker — though its credibility as a mediator is complicated by its own domestic crackdown and competing relationships with Moscow and Washington.

sections.heading: Canada-Turkey Nuclear Talks and Turkic State Diplomacy
sections.body:
Canadian Foreign Affairs Minister Anita Anand confirmed Canada is exploring nuclear energy cooperation with Turkey, including partnership on CANDU reactors, following discussions with her Turkish counterpart Hakan Fidan. Anand welcomed Fidan to Ottawa as the two countries discussed elevating bilateral ties to a strategic level ahead of the NATO summit. Turkey's state-run Anadolu Agency reported that Fidan said the two nations were deepening cooperation across key sectors, while the Globe and Mail reported that Turkey is interested in large-scale CANDU reactors. The talks remain in early stages, and Canada is also pursuing a free trade agreement with Turkey, according to Devdiscourse.

Meanwhile, Erdogan announced that Ankara will host the 13th summit of the Organization of Turkic States in the autumn, bringing together Turkey, Azerbaijan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and Uzbekistan, alongside observer members. TRT World reported Erdogan framing both the NATO and OTS summits as part of Ankara's elevation as a global diplomatic hub.
sections.whyItMatters: The nuclear talks with Canada represent a potential new dimension in Turkey's energy diversification and its effort to deepen partnerships beyond traditional Western and Middle Eastern ties. The Turkic States summit reinforces Ankara's parallel track of building influence in Central Asia.

sections.heading: Aegean Tensions Rise as Turkey Advances EEZ Legislation
sections.body:
Erdogan has drafted legislation — reportedly due to be presented to parliament this month — that would unilaterally declare a 200-nautical-mile exclusive economic zone for Turkey, according to the Irish Times. The move would conflict with Greece's own EEZ claims and, in theory, give Turkey control over fishing, oil and gas drilling, and even sovereignty claims over Greek islands close to the Turkish mainland. Turkey is not a party to the 1994 UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), which 169 countries have ratified.

Greek Foreign Minister George Gerapetritis warned of an 'extremely fragile geopolitical environment' at an EU foreign ministers meeting in May. Turkish Defense Minister Yasar Guler stated the Turkish army has 'the power and determination to neutralize every threat.' US Representatives Gus Bilirakis and Brad Schneider have sent a bipartisan letter to Secretary of State Marco Rubio calling for the US to publicly oppose Turkey's maritime claims.

Al-Monitor reported that Turkey has also warned Greece and Cyprus not to engage in an arms race, as both countries continue military buildup in the eastern Mediterranean.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's EEZ push converts the 'Blue Homeland' doctrine from military doctrine into legislation just weeks before hosting NATO allies — including Greece — at the Ankara summit, creating a direct diplomatic confrontation with a fellow alliance member.

bottomLine: Nine days before hosting 32 world leaders at the NATO summit, Turkey is simultaneously tightening domestic control and expanding its strategic reach — arresting 178 people in security sweeps that also netted journalists and environmentalists, denying press accreditation to dozens of journalists, and banning public gatherings for 13 days. The $700M US engine sale for the KAAN fighter is a concrete win for Ankara and a signal of warming US-Turkey ties, but it falls short of resolving the F-35/S-400 impasse. Erdogan is using the diplomatic convergence — Iran ceasefire diplomacy, Ukraine mediation offer, Canada nuclear talks, Turkic State summit planning — to project Turkey as an indispensable power broker, even as his Aegean EEZ legislation and domestic crackdown risk alienating the very allies he needs to impress at the summit.
sources: Reuters, Firstpost, Al-Monitor, Daily Sabah, Anadolu Agency, AP, Irish Times, Devdiscourse, Globe and Mail, Bloomberg, The Conversation, MLSA, TRT World, US News & World Report
